coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of blue emissive carbon quantum dots from Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower: Surface functionalization analysis by FT-IR spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Rojas-Valencia, O.G.
Regules-Carrasco, M.
Hernández-Fuentes, J.
Germán, C.M. Reza-San
Estrada-Flores, M.
Villagarcía-Chávez, E.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were obtained, for first time, from the Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower (Roselle flower) by an easy, economical, and eco-friendly single-step carbonization method in air atmosphere at different times and carbonization temperatures. All the obtained samples presented luminescent properties with blue color emission. The obtained CQDs at different temperatures and times of calcination were characterized by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique to determine the average hydrodynamic diameter, it was found that at 400°C the smallest particle size was 34 nm, which are considered as agglomerates. The obtained CQDs were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and compared with the Hibiscus Sabdariffa´s spectra to identify the functional groups that performed the surface passivation, and they allow the obtaining of luminescent properties; the identified groups were OCH and C-OH from xylose and glucose, which are part of the sambubioside group of anthocyanins (responsible the red coloration of the flower); -COOH, ortho-CO-C6H4-OH and =C-O-C. HRTEM technique showed monodisperse particles with approximate diameter of 10 nm and XRD demonstrate the samples composition of Carbon, with different crystalline system for samples synthesized at distinct conditions. The obtained CQDs at 200°C-1h (lower conditions), 300°C-3hrs (middle conditions) and 400°C-4hrs (higher conditions) were embedded into a thin PMMA film with a decrease in emissive intensity from CQDs with respect to embedded ones.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hibiscus sabdariffa
Color emission
Surface passivation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon quantum dots embedded into thin film of PMMA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CQDs obtained at 200, 300 and 400°C (A, B and C) and different carbonization times (1, 2, 3 and 4 hours).
Hydrodynamic average diameter of CQDs in logarithmic scale.
Particle size behavior at different temperatures and time of carbonization.
Functional groups which allowed surface passivation from CQDs.
HR-TEM of (A) CQDs obtained at 200°C during 1h (lower conditions) and 400°C during 4 h (higher conditions).
XRD results of CQDs obtained at lower and higher conditions of synthesis.
UV-Vis and PL spectra of CQDs obtained at 200°C-1hr, 300°C-3hrs and 400°C-4hrs
PL spectra of CQDs (solid line) and embedded ones into PMMA (dotted line). (A) embedded CQDs and (B) with blue emission, from left to right at 200°c-1h, 300°C-3h and 400°C-4h.
Crystallite size, obtained with Scherrer equation.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of blue emissive carbon quantum dots from Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower: Surface functionalization analysis by FT-IR spectroscopy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Address: 1190 Insurgentes Norte, Alcaldía Gustavo A. Madero, Capultitlán 07370, Ciudad de México.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 1190 Insurgentes Norte, Alcaldía Gustavo A. Madero

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were obtained, for first time, from the Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower (Roselle flower) by an easy, economical, and eco-friendly single-step carbonization method in air atmosphere at different times and carbonization temperatures. All the obtained samples presented luminescent properties with blue color emission. The obtained CQDs at different temperatures and times of calcination were characterized by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique to determine the average hydrodynamic diameter, it was found that at 400°C the smallest particle size was 34 nm, which are considered as agglomerates. The obtained CQDs were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and compared with the Hibiscus Sabdariffa´s spectra to identify the functional groups that performed the surface passivation, and they allow the obtaining of luminescent properties; the identified groups were OCH and C-OH from xylose and glucose, which are part of the sambubioside group of anthocyanins (responsible the red coloration of the flower); -COOH, ortho-CO-C6H4-OH and =C-O-C. HRTEM technique showed monodisperse particles with approximate diameter of 10 nm and XRD demonstrate the samples composition of Carbon, with different crystalline system for samples synthesized at distinct conditions. The obtained CQDs at 200°C-1h (lower conditions), 300°C-3hrs (middle conditions) and 400°C-4hrs (higher conditions) were embedded into a thin PMMA film with a decrease in emissive intensity from CQDs with respect to embedded ones.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also called carbon dots (CDs), are a relatively new type of fluorescent carbon-based materials, which is one of the most abundant chemical elements on surface of Earth, whereby they are not toxic as conventional quantum dots obtained from heavy metals such as cadmium: CdSe [1], CdTe [2] or lead: PbS [3], PbSe [4]. Nowadays, CQDs have gained special attention due to their chemical stability, excellent water solubility [5] and tunable fluorescence emission, because of their structure which consist of nanocrystalline cores with sp2 conjugated carbon embedded within an amorphous carbon matrix (sp3 hybridized) with many defects, probably as a result of surface oxidation [6, 7]. In contrast with inorganic quantum dots, CQDs possess low toxicity and low production costs and therefore they have different applications in biological field [6], optoelectronics [7], photovoltaics [8], biomedicine [9], trackable drug delivery carrier [10], chemical-sensing [11], among others.

For CQDs synthesis, two general routs have been reported: a) top-down approach which involve breaking of long carbon chains by different methods; however, drawbacks of this approach includes the requirement of expensive materials, harsh reaction conditions, long reaction time [12] and particles with unpredictable shape and size are obtained [13]; and b) bottom-up approach, where small carbon structures are converted to tunable size CQDs. Bottom-up approach allows use of different technics of synthesis such as hydrothermal treatment [14], ultrasonic treatment [15], pyrolysis [16], carbonization [17], solvothermal method [18], etc., in addition, a wide range of chemical precursors can be used such as poly(vinyl alcohol) [19], urea [20], graphite powder [21], trimesic acid-amino acetanilide [22], lactose [23], etc. The obtained CQDs are spherical or nearly spherically carbon nanoparticles whose surface is stabilized by some functional groups [24], however, some studies have reported triangular structures [25]. Light emission in CQDs is not by size-quantization effect, such as in semiconductor quantum dots, an alternative explanation supposes that the emission of CQDs comes from the functional molecules groups attached to their surface (surface passivation) [27, 28]. This hypothesis is supported by the dependence between QCDs emission and surface oxidation [26], which means that degree of surface oxidation controls the surface state of CQDs tunable emission properties.

Frequently, luminescent CQDs are obtained by the surface functionalization of the carbon nanoparticles with hydrocarbon molecules, which it involves the use of toxic solvents [30]. Nowadays, a green synthetic approach has been explored by using natural sources, such as biomass, for CQDs synthesis. The great advantage of using green synthesis is that employs eco-friendly compounds and non-toxic with the ability to up production volume [31].

In recent years, use of waste biomass has been exploited for green synthesis of CQDs. Different natural sources have been reported, such as, potato starch [28], grass [29], leaves [30], fruits [31], vegetables [32], cereals [33], etc. Another of the main advantages of using biomass for CQDs synthesis is that, after obtaining material, a functionalization process should not be carried out due to the presence of many organic compounds in the natural source, which makes the obtaining process easier, cheaper, and using of toxic solvents for obtaining emissive CQDs is eliminated.

In this work, we report for first time the obtaining of CQDs from Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower by carbonization approach. Hibiscus Sabdariffa, also called roselle flower, is a shrub belonging to the family Malvaceae with red flowers in a form of calyces [34]. Calyces have been found to be rich in phenolic compounds such as citric acid, hydroxy citric acid, flavonoids such as quercetin, luteolin and glycosides, anthocyanins, responsible of red color, such as cyanidin-3-glucoside, delphinidin-3-glucoside, cyanidin-3-sambubioside and delphinidin-3-sambubioside [39]. The main purpose of this work is to study the organic functional groups present in Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower, that allow the surface functionalization of emissive CQDs, obtained at different times of carbonization.

Dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers were obtained at the local market. They were used without any pre-washing to preserve all the organic compounds and enhance the surface functionalization. Distilled water was employed as dispersant. To get the powder of CQDs a Whatman filter paper (0.22 μm pore diameter) was employed.

In a batch carbonization process, 5 g of dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers were placed in a melting pot and carbonized into a muffle under air atmosphere. Different samples were obtained at 200, 300 and 400°C and 1, 2, 3 and 4 hours of calcination. After every carbonization process, the black product obtained was mechanically pulverized to get a fine powder and dispersed in distillated water. The dark solution was centrifugated at 4000 rpm for 30 minutes and the supernatant solution, which contained the CQDs, was filtered with a filter paper.
The CQDs were characterized by Dynamic Light Scattering (LDS) and Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy techniques.

This technique was employed to determine the size distributions and hydrodynamic average diameters (Dh) of the particles. For this analysis, a sample of QCDs dispersed in distillated water was placed into a quartz cell and measurement was carried out at room temperature using a ZETASIZER Nano-ZSP of Marvel Instruments equipped with a green laser at λ = 532 nm.

This technique was used to identify the functional groups that allow the surface passivation on CQDs to get luminescent properties. Different powder samples of CQDs were placed on an ATR accessory from Perkin Elmer spectrometer “Spectrum Two” brand and analyzed from 4000 to 1000 cm−1.
TEM was used to observe morphological shape and inter lattice spacing. A JEM-ARM200CF, JEOL spectrometer operating at 200 kV was used.

XRD was used to determine the crystalline phases and the different compounds in the samples. A Bruker D 8 Phaser diffractometer was used, with linxeye detector and Cu Kα radiation (l= 1.54Å), measurements were performed with a scanning geometry of 10° to 70° using of 0.02° and a rate of 2.4 degrees per minute.
This technique was used to observe the maximum absorption peak of samples, using a double beam UV-Vis Perkin Elmer-Lambda 365 spectrometer with a (1.0 cm) quartz cell from 300 to 600 nm.

The photoluminescence signal was dispersed at room temperature by a spex 500 M monochromator with focal length 0.5 m and detected by Hamamatsu GaAs photomultiplier tube (PMT) model R493-02. A He-Cd laser (325 nm) with 50 mW power was used for excitation.

The CQDs, were dispersed in distilled water and exposed to a UV lamp irradiation (254 nm) in order to observe if they had emission light properties. Fig. 1 shows the samples at different carbonization time and temperature. Sample “A” was obtained at 200°C, while “B” and “C” were obtained at 300 and 400°C, respectively; the number associated to each image (1, 2, 3 and 4) refers to the time, in hours, of the carbonization process. Like the figure shows, all samples had light emission in blue color, which means CQDs were obtained successfully.

For hydrodynamic average diameter results, the obtained CQDs at 200°C, 300°C and 400°C during 1, 2, 3 and 4 hours of carbonization were analyzed. Fig. 2 shows the unimodal distributions of hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) which ones were estimated from the diffusivity coefficient determined by DLS [40]. It can be shown that as the calcination temperature is higher, a lower average diameter is obtained: 137, 80 and 50 nm. for the samples A1, B1 and C1, carbonized during 1 h; 127, 78 and 45 nm. corresponding to the samples carbonized 2 hours A2, B2 and C2, 110, 72 and 39 nm. for the samples A3, B3 and C3, carbonized for 3 hours; finally, 105, 70 and 34 nm. for A4, B4 and C4 samples, respectively for the samples carbonized for 4 hours. The results have the same trend at any time.

A summary of the previous analysis is shown in Fig. 3 , where is possible to observe the distribution of particle size at different temperature and time of carbonization, both variables are responsible of the difference in the products obtained, this behavior could be explained to a higher temperature causes a higher carbonization degree of Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower and consequently with the help of mechanical crushing, a lower average diameter is obtained. Similarly, it is observed that lowest size distribution is obtained at 400°C, followed by the obtained samples at 300 and 200°C, which can also be attributed to the carbonization degree. The smallest particles were obtained at higher temperature and a longer time of exposure to heat.

To identify the functional groups which allowed surface passivation of CQDs and their luminescence properties obtaining, the FTIR spectra of Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower and CQDs samples obtained at different time and temperature of calcination were analyzed and compared. Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of CQDs obtained at 1, 2, 3 and 4 hours of carbonization time at 200, 300 and 400°C. It can be shown that for Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower, a signal is observed at 3400 cm−1 due to -OH stretching vibrations of the hydroxyl group [35,36] which suggests the presence of the anthocyanins cyanidin-3-O-sambubioside and delphinidin-3-O-sambusbioside [37]; likewise, the signal that appears at 2900 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 are due to the vibrations of the C-H3 and C-H2 bonds [38] of lipidic carbohydrates; the signals shown at 1793 and 1730 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C=O bonds of fatty acids [39]; the stretching vibration signal perceived at 1018 cm−1 [40] corresponds to the vibration of the aromatic rings and to the group =C-O-C of the flavonoids (constituents of anthocyanins), while the signal at 1600 cm−1 is due to the vibration of the bonds in the ortho-CO-C6H4-OH group [41]. A weak signal is observed at 1400 and 1202 cm−1 due to the OCH and C-OH bonds of the sambubioside group from anthocyanins [42] and the signal shown at 875 cm−1 is due to the bond of CO. For FTIR spectra from the obtained CQDs, it can be shown that, the signal corresponding to -OH group decreased, which suggests that the carbonization of the Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower was carried out effectively and, consequently, the thermal degradation of anthocyanins. The disappearance of the signal corresponding to the C=O group (1793 and 1730 cm−1) validates the thermal degradation of the fatty acids present in the Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower.

It can be observed that as the temperature and time carbonization increase, the signal at 1400 cm−1 increases, which indicates that OCH and C-OH functional groups from xylose and glucose (sambubioside group) from the anthocyanins allowed the surface passivation, as well as, the increase in signal at 875 from carboxylic group and ortho-CO-C6H4-OH group at 1600 cm−1 and the permanence of =C-O-C group at 1018 cm−1 [43], also allowing the surface passivation of the luminescent QCDs.

Samples obtained at 200°C during 1h (lower conditions) and 400°C during 4 h (higher conditions) were characterized by HR-TEM. In Fig. 5 can be shown carbonized dots structures and mono disperse with average diameter of 10 nm. Samples shows a 0.36 and 0.25 nm lattice spacing (004 and 111 lattices plane of carbon) for lower and higher conditions samples respectively [44,45]. HR-TEM revealed that at higher reaction times and temperature, exists a better arrangements carbon atom.

The samples synthesized at the lower and higher conditions were characterized by XRD to determine the phases of Carbon and other compounds in them, before the filtration process. The results of XDR for the sample calcinated at 200°C during 1h. are shown in Fig. 6 . were semicrystalline products are observed, the XRD characteristics reflections of the Carbon in graphite phase are appeared at 2Ө 26.61°, 43.45°, 46.32°, 54.81° and 63.67°, agree with the 75-2078 card, where a rhombohedral crystal system is reported, R-3m (166) space group and lattice parameters: a=b=c=3.635
A ˙
with angles α=β=ϒ=39.49°, the major peak of the CQDs was found at 26.611° with hkl=(111). The residual peaks are attributed to calcium oxalate hydrate as 03-0087 XRD card indicates. While the sample synthesized at 400°C for 4 hours shown a crystalline diffractogram, it has characteristic reflections in 26.6°, 42.46°, 43.01°, 44.67°, 47.30° and 50.83° corresponding to Carbon with hexagonal crystal system, space group P63mc (186), with lattice parameters: a=b=2.4560
A ˙
and c=13.392
A ˙
with angles α=β=90° and ϒ=120° as can be found in the 26-1080 diffraction card; Calcium Carbonate are attributed to the rest of peaks in diffractogram agree with 72-1214 card; the most intense signals was found at 26.6° with hkl=(004). In both samples, calcium-based structures were found, this is due to the Hibiscus Sabdariffa flower composition, with high content of this element [46–48]. As clearly can be observed, more symmetrical structures are obtained at higher conditions for both, carbon and calcium-based compounds, also more crystalline products are obtained at high condition of temperature and time. TEM confirms the results found in this section for both samples.

The results of crystallite size for both samples are showed in table 1 , they were obtained with Scherrer equation (Eq. 1):
(1)
L =
K λ
β c o s
Θ
Where, L is the size of crystallite in nm., λ is the X-ray wavelength (for Cu: 1.5406
A ˙
= 0.15406 nm), β is the peak width of the diffraction peak profile at half maximum height in radians and K is a constant related to crystallite shape, normally taken as 0.9. The θ can be in degrees or radians. [49]

Since XRD patterns were obtained before filtration, the crystallite size is bigger than the CQDs, but were possible to identify the phase of Carbon in the samples and nanometric values were determined, however, TEM analysis was measured in filtered and dispersed CQDs, were is possible to observe the real size of the nanoparticles.

The optical absorption peaks of samples obtained at 200°C-1hr, 300°C-3hrs and 400°C-4hrs (dotted line) and their emission spectra (solid line) are shown in Fig. 7 . For excitation spectra a weak peak is presented at 350 nm which is attributed to the n-π* transitions of C=O bonds [50, 51]. The emission peaks appear at higher wavelength from excitation ones, this phenomenon is known as bathochromic shift, which is distinctive from semiconductor and carbon quantum dots [52]. The emission wavelength appears at 371, 390 and 413 nm for samples obtained at 400°C-4hrs, 200°C-1hr and 300°C-3hrs, respectively, which correspond with blue emission color [53, 54] and it is validated with Fig 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots embedded into thin film of PMMA
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs obtained were embedded into a thin film of poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) as a potential application in photoactive functional film on solar cells. As-obtained CQDs at 200°C-1hr, 300°C-3hrs and 400°C-4hrs were mixed with 50 wt% PMMA in chloroform (10 mg of CQDs in 10 mL of 50 wt% PMMA/chloroform and 1 mL of acetic acid). The resulting mixture was sonicated for 10 min in ultrasonic bath and dispersed over a glass holder sample. Fig. 8 shows emission spectra for CQDs (solid line) and embedded into PMMA (dotted line), it can be shown that sample with the highest and lowest emission intensity are the obtained at 400°C-4hrs and 300°C-3hrs, respectively. There is a decrease in emission intensity from CQDs to embedded ones as the peak red shifts, this could be due to some polymeric matrix effect or amount of CQDs particles, which could result in quenching or modification of the emissive intensity. Also, in Fig. 8, it can be shown CQDs embedded into PMMA excited with and without UV beam at 325 nm with blue emission, respectively. There is no visible macrophase separation between PMM and CQDs embedded, which means a good quality of their dispersion.

CQDs of different sizes with luminescent properties were obtained by the calcination approach. The obtained CQDs possess excellent colloidal suspension in water, luminescence properties with light emission in blue color with smallest average diameter size, according with DLS, at higher calcination temperature (34 nm at 400°C of carbonization temperature and 4 hrs). FTIR analysis indicates that functional groups (OCH and C-OH) from xylose and glucose, -COOH, ortho-CO-C6H4-OH and =C-O-C allowed the surface passivation from CQDs, and for hence luminescent properties. HRTEM showed monodispersed particles with approximate diameter of 10 nm, which means that DLS obtained diameter is attributed to CQDs agglomerates. XRD demonstrate the samples composition of Carbon, with rhombohedral system for the sample synthesized at 1 hour and 200°C (75-2078), while hexagonal crystal system for the sample synthesized at 400°C for 4 hours (26-1080). Photoluminescence analysis shows that QCDs obtained at 400°C-hrs have the higher intensity emission. PMMA was used to embed the QCDs obtained into a thin film with luminescent properties lower than CQDs without polymer.
This study suggests a new organic carbon source for CQDs obtaining by an economical and easy method.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge to the Nanotechnology Fundamentals Laboratory from ESIQIE/IPN for providing the experimentation place and IPN for the SIP project 20201887.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent nano-biomass dots : ultrasonic- assisted extraction and their application as nanoprobe for Fe 3+ detection
